title: Bṛhad Ajitaśānti Stava (The Great Ajita-Śānti Hymn — Ninth Smaraṇa)
summary: This is a Sanskrit hymn of praise that worships two Tīrthaṅkaras together — Ajitanātha (the 2nd) and Śāntinātha (the 16th) — as a single radiant 'pair.' Across seventeen verses it retells their lives, from the auspicious dreams before their birth, through their princely reigns, renunciation, enlightenment, and final liberation. It is recited as a protective and merit-bringing prayer: the hymn itself promises that whoever devoutly recites it on the holy days is shielded from fear, disease, and misfortune, and gains lasting well-being.
ritualPlacement: It is the ninth and last of the Nine Smaraṇas (Nava Smaraṇa), the fixed set of protective stotras recited within the Pākṣika (Pakkhī) pratikramaṇa, after the two Nandī-sūtra sajjhāys and before the Pakkhī Khāmaṇā. Because Cāumāsī (Cāturmāsika) and Sāṁvatsarī have no independent text of their own — they follow the Pākṣika order with only the word-substitution 'pakkhī → cāumāsī / saṁvatsari' and a longer kāyotsarga — this same smaraṇa recurs unchanged in those two pratikramaṇas as well. It is not one of the six Āvaśyakas; it belongs to the protective-recitation portion of the Pakkhī observance.
authorAndSource: — The authoritative tradition knowledge base for this Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) book does not name the author of the ninth (Bṛhad) Ajitaśānti Stava, unlike its companions in the set (the Prakrit Ajitaśānti of Nandiṣeṇasūri is the 2nd Smaraṇa; the Laghu Ajitaśānti of Vīragaṇin is the 8th). This 'Bṛhad' (great/extended) version is the longer Sanskrit hymn of the three Ajita-Śānti compositions. Authorship is therefore left uncertain here rather than guessed (see notes).
structureArc: The seventeen verses form a joint life-story (pañca-kalyāṇaka) of the two Jinas, praised side by side: vv.1–2 salute Ajita and then Śānti individually ('naumi jinam ajitam' / 'śāntim upaśānta-bhava-bhaya'); v.3 binds them as a single 'pair of Jina-lords' (jinapatī ajitaśāntī) invoked for prosperity. v.4 evokes the joy of their mothers at the auspicious dreams (elephant, bull, etc.) heralding their conception; v.5 the gods' birth-festival on Mount Mandara (Meru). vv.6–7 give their distinguishing details — Ajita: Ayodhyā/Kosalā, father Jitaśatru, golden-hued, elephant emblem; Śānti: Hastināpura/Gajapura, the Viśvasena line, deer emblem, called Gāṅgeya. v.8 recalls their long, wonder-working reigns; v.9 their year of giving (vārṣika-dāna) and renunciation (vrata/dīkṣā). v.10 the dawning of omniscience (kevala-jñāna), beside which even the sun is a mere firefly; vv.11–13 the assembly that gathers to worship — the rushing saṅgha, the nectar of teaching that cools souls burning in saṁsāra, and the divine marks of an Arhat (three white umbrellas, chowries, sounding drum). v.14 their passage to the 'stainless abode' (nirvāṇa) and the worshipper's vow to adore them; v.15 the protective promise that calamities cannot harm the devotee of the pair; v.16 the explicit fruit-of-recitation (phala-śruti) for the pākṣika, cāturmāsika, and vārṣika holy days; v.17 a closing benediction that this Jina-pair bring joy to the Saṅgha.
recurringImagery: The governing image is the two Tīrthaṅkaras as a single 'twin' or 'pair' (yamala) — Ajita and Śānti are never separated in the worship, even though they lived ages apart and in different cities; the hymn deliberately yokes their two biographies into one parallel telling. A second pervasive feature is the dense Sanskrit sound-play (yamaka and anuprāsa — repeated syllables and chiming alliteration, e.g. 'ajitam ajitam uditoditam'), so the verses are meant to be heard as music as much as read for meaning. A third recurring motif is light and radiance: omniscience is the sun before which the day's sun is only a firefly (v.10), and the closing verses speak of the pair as 'resplendent' and 'bright-rayed.' Finally vv.11–13 paint the standard samavasaraṇa scene with the eight divine attributes (aṣṭa-prātihārya) — the threefold umbrella, the waving chowries, and the celestial drum — which the verses simply name, relying on this shared explanation.
keyThemes: Joint worship of Ajitanātha and Śāntinātha as one Jina-pair
The five auspicious events (pañca-kalyāṇaka) of a Tīrthaṅkara's life
The samavasaraṇa and the eight divine marks of an Arhat
Protection from fear, disease, and calamity

glossary.definition: A hymn of praise; a devotional poem extolling a Jina or Jinas.
A 'remembrance' — a protective stotra recited for spiritual recollection and safeguarding; here the ninth of the fixed Nine Smaraṇas.
The 2nd Tīrthaṅkara of this age; golden-hued, born at Ayodhyā (Kosalā) to King Jitaśatru, with the elephant as emblem.
The 16th Tīrthaṅkara; born at Hastināpura (Gajapura) in the Viśvasena line, with the deer (antelope) as emblem; also a universal monarch (cakravartin).
A pair or twin; here the device of worshipping the two Jinas Ajita and Śānti together as one inseparable unit.
The five auspicious life-events of every Tīrthaṅkara: conception, birth, renunciation, omniscience, and liberation — the narrative spine of this hymn.
Absolute, all-knowing omniscience attained on the path to liberation; in v.10 likened to a sun beside which the ordinary sun is a firefly.
The divine preaching-assembly an Arhat addresses after enlightenment, to which gods and beings rush (vv.11–13).
The eight divine attributes manifesting around an Arhat — including the three white umbrellas (ātapatra-traya), the chowries (cāmara), and the celestial drum (dundubhi) named in v.13.
The year-long giving of gifts a Tīrthaṅkara distributes before renouncing the world (v.9).
The 'stainless' liberated state; the final abode (dhāma) the two Jinas reach in v.14.
The 'declaration of fruit' — the verse(s) stating the benefits of reciting the hymn; here v.16, promising well-being to those who recite it on the holy days.
A Sanskrit poetic figure of repeated syllable-sequences with differing sense; pervasive in this hymn and central to its musical effect, alongside anuprāsa (alliteration).

howToRead: A newcomer can read this simply as the braided life-story of two beloved Jinas, Ajita and Śānti, ending in a heartfelt prayer for protection and joy — follow the verse-by-verse arc above and let the chiming sound carry you. An advanced practitioner should attend to the deliberate yamaka/anuprāsa craftsmanship and the precise pañca-kalyāṇaka and prātihārya references, noting that the per-verse commentaries assume the framing, imagery, and glossary established here and will not repeat them.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Ritual placement of the Nine Smaraṇas within the Pākṣika pratikramaṇa, the shared-sutra substitution rule for Cāumāsī/Sāṁvatsarī, and the verbatim verse text.

notes: Authorship of this ninth (Bṛhad) Ajitaśānti Stava is left as unknown ('—') because the authoritative tradition knowledge base does not attribute it, and an open web search returned an unverifiable/likely-fabricated attribution and sample verse that should not be trusted. The biographical details (cities, parents, emblems, Gāṅgeya epithet for Śānti) are read directly from the verses and accord with standard Jain accounts of the 2nd and 16th Tīrthaṅkaras. The verse text mixes Sanskrit with the book's own orthographic readings (e.g. the spelling 'atiyār' convention noted in the source book); the book is the golden source for every mātrā and conjunct, and apparent irregularities should be preserved, not normalized. The single source URL is given as the tradition's general site; if a stable per-page citation is required it should be verified before being relied upon.
